data.title: Optiscan Imaging Launches New Medical Imaging Device For Pathology; Shares Up 7%
data.description:
Optiscan Imaging revealed its next-generation microscopic medical imaging device InForm specifically designed for pathology workflows, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian bourse.

The device can be used in a variety of pathology settings, most notably in laboratory medicine, pathology practice, point-of-care, and digital pathology, the filing added.

The new device expands the company into the pathology market, per the filing.

Company shares rose almost 7% in recent Wednesday trade.

data.title: Indian Pharma stocks drop on US tariff fears
data.description:
** India's Nifty pharma index slides 2% after the U.S. President Donald Trump said he intends to levy import tariffs of 25% or more on pharmaceuticals

** NIPHARM drops most among 13-major sectoral indexes; heavyweights Sun Pharma , Dr. Reddy's and Cipla down 1.7%, 3.8% and 1.74%, respectively

** 18 of the 20 index constituents are trading in red

** SUN, REDY, CIPL among top drags on the benchmark Nifty 50 , which is down 0.1%

** Indian drugmakers derive about 31% of their revenue from the U.S.

** NIPHARM hits lowest in seven months, it has dropped ~15% since all-time high in October 9

data.title: Hubtown Makes Additional INR107 Million Investment in Subsidiary Rare Townships
data.description:
Hubtown has increased its shareholding in its subsidiary Rare Townships to 73% from 68% through an investment of 107.1 million Indian rupees, according to a Tuesday filing with the Indian stock exchanges.

For the investment, Rare Townships allotted 10,705,606 Class A equity shares of face value of 10 rupees each to Hubtown.

Rare Townships is engaged in the real estate business of construction and development of residential and commercial premises.

Shares of the company were down over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: SGX Sicom RSS3 Rubber Untraded at Midday
SGX Sicom Benchmark March TSR20 Rubber Down 1.8 Cents at 202.0 U.S. Cents/Kg
South Korea's Automobile Exports Hits $5 Billion in January Despite Production Decline
data.description:
South Korea's automobile exports hit the second highest in January, reaching $5 billion in the month, despite a drop in production and domestic sales, the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy said Tuesday.

Auto production declined 18.9% to 290,590 units, impacted by a plant's temporary shutdown for electric vehicle transition, while domestic sales fell 9% to 105,928 units, the release said.

Hybrid electric vehicle exports surged 35.5% year-on-year to 35,701 units, though export value declined 19.6% amidst a high base effect and fewer working days, it said.

data.title: HSBC Plans $2 Billion Share Repurchase Program
data.description:
HSBC plans to launch a share buyback program for up to $2 billion, set to complete with the release of the company's first-quarter financial results.

The program is part of the British lender's plan to return $26.9 billion to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends, according to a Wednesday release.

data.title: BOJ's Takata Calls for Gradual Rate Hikes, Sees Upside Risks to Prices — Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--The Bank of Japan needs to keep adjusting the degree of monetary accommodation, policy board member Hajime Takata said Wednesday, as he warned about the risk of inflation climbing too high.

Takata also expressed optimism about the outlook for the U.S. economy despite growing worries about the impact of President Trump's policies, saying strength in the U.S. could buoy Japan's economy too.

"It is important to shift gears gradually--even after the additional interest-rate hike in January--to avoid the risks of price upswings and financial overheating from becoming a reality," Takata said in a speech to business leaders in Japan's northern prefecture of Miyagi.

Takata said that further rate hikes would be possible if corporate activity--including the passing on of costs to consumers, wage increases and capital spending--continues, and if the bank's economic outlook is progressively realized.

The Japanese central bank raised the policy rate to 0.5% in late January and has pledged to seek more interest-rate hikes if the economy and prices develop in line with its forecasts.

Many economists expect the BOJ's next tightening move to come around the summer time so that it can sustain the pace of one hike in every six months. In a reflection of those hopes, the yen has stopped weakening against the dollar, and Japanese government bond yields have reached multi-year highs.

Turning to the issue of U.S. policy risks, Takata, a veteran market economist before joining the BOJ, said that improvement in the U.S. economy would brighten the outlook for Japan.

Though attention needs to be paid to uncertainties around the new U.S. administration's policymaking, Takata said he believes the possibility of a re-acceleration in the U.S. economy, rather than a soft landing, is rising.

"If overseas economies, particularly the U.S., perform better, the momentum of [Japan's economic] recovery may strengthen," Takata said. Comfort over the outlook for the world's largest economy could help reduce market volatility, the board member said, giving the Japanese central bank more flexibility in its policy-making.

That said, if strength in the U.S. economy causes the yen to weaken and Japanese companies give big pay increases this year, prices could pick up faster than expected in Japan, he added.

His view contrasts with growing concerns about the negative economic impact of Trump's tariff threats. The U.S. president on Tuesday said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles, a move that would likely have a significant impact on Japan, given that a sizable amount of its exports to the U.S. are auto-related.

Data earlier showed that Japan's exports grew for a fourth consecutive month in January but trade protectionism could hinder what has been a key engine of growth for the economy. Shipments to the U.S. rose 8.1% on the year in the month, in part due to solid demand for cars.

The U.S. was the standout destination for Japan's exports, but the jump may be "the calm before the Trumpian storm," ING economist Min Joo Kang said in a note.

Tariffs on auto exports would be particularly damaging for Japan's economy as the sector accounts for 15% of industrial production and spillover effects are remarkably high due to the intertwined nature of the domestic supply chain, Oxford Economics said in a recent report.

The U.S. also has the upper hand in potentially using tariffs as a means to gain diplomatic leverage, as shipments to the U.S. account for 20% of Japan's exports while Japan only makes up 5% of U.S. imports, said economist Makoto Tsuchiya.

"Even in the absence of bilateral tariffs on Japan, globalized supply chains mean Japan could still be hit by tariffs on other economies," he said.

The Japanese government has been closely communicating with the U.S. administration on tariffs. Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba said Monday that Japan differs from other countries because it is making a lot of investments in the U.S., creating jobs and contributing to wage increases for American workers.

"We are not asking for special treatment for Japan, but we will continue to demonstrate the legitimacy of our claims," Ishiba said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Mr. D.I.Y.'s Growth Prospects Likely Intact Despite Short-Term Weakness — Market Talk
data.description: Mr. D.I.Y. Group (M)'s long-term growth may remain intact, despite short-term earnings facing pressure from warehouse issues and declining sales, CGS International analysts Lew Cheng Wei and Prem Jearajasingam say in a note. Concerns over weak 4Q 2024 results have driven the company's share price down 20% year-to-date, they note. However, store expansions, stable margins, improved consumer sentiment, and new retail brand KKV's contributions should support long-term growth. Positive macroeconomic factors, including wage hikes should boost consumer spending and sales growth in 2025, they add. CGS International maintains an add rating on Mr. D.I.Y. Group and keeps target price at MYR2.60. Shares are 2.0% lower at MYR1.47.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Vinci Logs Higher Autoroutes, Airport Traffic in January
data.description:
Vinci said Tuesday that its intercity networks and passenger traffic grew year over year in January.

The French concessions and construction company unit Vinci Autoroutes logged a 9.5% year-over-year growth in its intercity network traffic in the reporting month.

Meanwhile, Vinci Airports posted a 9.2% year-over-year increase in passenger traffic in January.

data.title: Cordlife Flags Loss for 2024
data.description:
Cordlife expects to book a loss for the year ended Dec. 31, 2024, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Tuesday.

The company has attributed this to suspension of Singapore operations and ban on collection, testing, processing of new cord blood and human tissues and conditional resumption of operatins in a controlled manner.

The company expects revenue to improve during 2025 and will release its financial results on or before March 1.

data.title: HSBC Posts Higher FY24 Profit; Revenue Declines
data.description:
HSBC said Wednesday that profit for full-year 2024 increased, while revenue decreased year over year.

Profit attributable to ordinary shareholders of the parent company for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was $22.92 billion, compared with $22.43 billion earlier. EPS moved to $1.24 from $1.14.

The British lender's revenue was $65.85 billion, compared with $66.06 billion a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board approved a fourth interim dividend of $0.36 per share for the fourth quarter, payable April 25 to shareholders on record March 7. It paid $0.31 per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to $0.87 per share, up from $0.61 in the previous year.

For 2025, the lender expects banking net interest income of $42 billion.

HSBC Posts Quarterly Net Profit, Plans $2.0 Billion Share Buyback
data.description:

(04:03 GMT) *HSBC Holdings 4Q Net $197M Vs. Loss $153M >0005.HK

(04:06 GMT) *HSBC CEO: We Are Creating a Simple, More Agile, Focused Bank Built on Core Strengths >0005.HK
(04:08 GMT) *HSBC Expects Banking Net Interest Income of Around $42.0B in 2025 >0005.HK
(04:10 GMT) *HSBC Aims to Generate About $300M in Cost Reductions in 2025 >0005.HK
(04:11 GMT) *HSBC: Commited to Achieving Annualized Cost Base Reduction of $1.5B by End-2026 >0005.HK
(04:12 GMT) *HSBC: Expect to Incur Severance, Other Upfront Costs of $1.8B Over 2025, 2026 >0005.HK

(04:16 GMT) *HSBC: Total Dividend Payout for 2024 to Be 87c/Share >0005.HK
(04:26 GMT) HSBC Posts Quarterly Net Profit, Plans $2.0 Billion Share Buyback

By Kosaku Narioka

HSBC Holdings reported a quarterly net profit, supported by higher fee income, and said it plans to start a share buyback of up to $2.0 billion.

The London-based bank said Wednesday that it booked net profit of $197 million for the three months ended December, compared with a net loss of $153 million a year ago. That beat the estimated net profit of $27.9 million in a poll of analysts by Visible Alpha.

The bank's bottom line took a hit in the year-earlier period as it wrote down the value of its stake in major Chinese lender Bank of Communications by $3 billion.

Net interest income--the difference between interest earned on loans and that paid on deposits-- fell to $8.185 billion from $8.28 billion a year earlier, while net fee income climbed to $2.98 billion from $2.76 billion.

Chief Executive Georges Elhedery has been taking steps to simplify the bank's structure after taking the top job in early September.

Under his leadership, the bank merged its commercial- and investment-banking operations, cut the size of its executive committee by a third and plans to stop advising companies on doing deals and going public in the U.S. and Europe.

Elhedery is pushing the bank to focus on its strengths: retail banking in the U.K. and Hong Kong, acting as a bridge for large companies to global markets for their goods and services, and helping wealthy clients with their finances.

He wants to cut costs and speed up decision-making by trimming jobs across HSBC's 200,000-plus staff and criss-crossing layers of management.

Because of its role in straddling Western and Chinese markets, the lender is potentially exposed to trade and geopolitical tensions between the Trump administration and Beijing.

HSBC's shares in Hong Kong have risen 15% so far this year through Tuesday, following a 23% gain in 2024, partly driven by hopes for restructuring efforts under the new management led by Elhedery.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Light & Wonder's Australian shares soar on buying Grover Gaming's charitable gaming assets
data.description:
** Light & Wonder's Australia-listed shares (LWN.AX) rise 10.3% to a record high of A$169.57

** Stock top gainer on the benchmark index

** U.S.-based gaming co to acquire Grover Gaming's charitable gaming assets for $850 million

** Says deal is highly accretive to value and earnings, with high single-digit adjusted NPATA per-share accretion

** Nearly 319,000 shares change hands, 3.6 times the 30-day average volume of shares

** Stock up 12.1% this year, as of last close

data.title: Palm Oil Rebounds Strongly
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures surged near 2.0% to around 4,590 per tonne, bouncing back from losses in the prior session amid supply concerns.

Malaysia’s meteorological agency forecasts heavy rainfall in key palm-growing states until February 25, potentially disrupting harvests and transport.

Meanwhile, worker productivity is expected to decline during Ramadan and the subsequent festive season.

In Indonesia, the government is studying a biodiesel blend with 50% palm oil for 2026 and a 3% jet fuel blend.

However, weak exports capped gains, with Malaysian shipments falling 12.3%-19.9% in the first 15 days of February.

Meanwhile, India’s Solvent Extractors Association urged New Delhi to accelerate the "National Mission on Edible Oils" to boost domestic oilseed production.

On the tariff front, US President Trump is set to announce new duties on automobiles, chips, and drugs by April 2, adding to earlier tariffs.

These measures have caused persistent market distortions, including for palm oil.

data.title: HSBC 2024 profit beats estimates; announces $2 billion share buyback
data.description:
HSBC reported a 6.6% rise in annual profit on Wednesday, beating market expectations, as income withstood the impact of falling interest rates during a year in which sweeping restructuring pushed up costs.

The London-based bank reported profit before tax for 2024 of $32.3 billion. That compared with $30.3 billion a year earlier and the $31.7 billion average of analyst estimates compiled by the bank.

The Asia-focused bank also announced a new $2 billion share buyback which it plans to complete before next earnings in May.

The bank said it aimed to generate about $300 million of cost reductions in 2025, with a commitment to an annualised reduction of $1.5 billion in cost base expected by the end of 2026.

HSBC said it was aiming for a performance target of a mid-teens return on average tangible equity for each of the three years from 2025 to 2027, while noting the outlook for interest rates remains volatile and uncertain in the medium term.

The results come as CEO Georges Elhedery has embarked on costly restructuring just the outlook for lending revenue is muddied by divergence in central bank interest rate policies, with the euro zone having room to cut rates, the U.S. holding steady and Japan expected to raise.

data.title: Amlak Finance Seeks Shareholder Nod for Assets Sale
data.description:
Amlak Finance (DFM:AMLAK) called on a shareholder meeting to approve the sale of some of its investment properties, according to a Tuesday release.

The Emirati real estate financing company intends to sell the Ras Al Khor plots and other unspecified assets, pending regulatory approval.

data.title: New Zealand Shares Slip 0.1% as RBNZ Cuts Cash Rate — Market Talk
data.description: New Zealand's NZX-50 closed 0.1% lower at 13033.36, falling for a second consecutive session of narrow declines, as the country's central bank slashed the cash rate by another 50 basis points. The benchmark index gave back earlier gains as the Reserve Bank of New Zealand also warned of rising potential for international trade tensions, slower global growth and geopolitical shocks. With the cash rate at 3.75%, the RBNZ has cut by 175 bps since mid-2024. Property and aged-care stocks were mixed, while consumer-related shares were weak. Retailer Hallenstein Glasson shed 0.9% and casino operator SkyCity fell 2.0%. Pharmaceutical supplier Ebos was the worst performer, falling 4.9% after announcing the retirement of its CEO. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Crypto Has Moved Past FTX, But Still Needs 24/7 Risk Management, Brevan Howard's CIO Believes
data.description:
The crypto ecosystem has come a long way since the implosion of Sam Bankman Fried's FTX destroyed billions in investor wealth in 2023. However, the industry as a whole needs to more to become bullet proof, said TradFi experts at the "Views From Wall Street to Crypto" event held at Consensus Hong Kong on Wednesday.

"You have traditional players who have come into the space now, especially for us, most of our trading happens of exchange settlement, where you actually keep your assets on custodians while you are able to trade on exchanges," Gautam Sharma, CEO and CIO of Brevan Howard said. "So the technology has come far ahead in terms of the last 18 months since then, [but] there's more work to do."

Sharma stressed the need for 24/7 risk management, including market, counterparty, and credit risks.

Counterparty risk refers to the possibility of one party involved in a transaction failing to meet its obligation, resulting in a loss to the other party. This type of risk is higher in crypto than in traditional finance, given the absence of intermediaries such as banks or clearing houses that ensure trust and settlements, and it is a cause of concern for both directional and non-directional arbitration players.

"When we do arbitrage, the counterparty risk is the most important one," Fabio Frontini, founder of Abraxas Capital Management, said, adding that credit risk is also very important.

Frontini stressed the importance of simulating stress testing scenarios, referring to the perpetual futures market where users can lose the margin when stopped out on a trade, which is not the case in traditional markets. "It [stress testing] can be very rewarding, when done properly," Frontini added.

Mike Kuehnel, CEO of the market-making firm Flow Traders, highlighted the need to make innovation transparent to win over investor confidence and ensure "availability of data and moving liquidity without fragmentation around it."

"Getting the best price and giving you the possibility to transact whenever you want to is a key ingredient," Kuehnel added.

Liquidity, or the ability of the market to absorb large orders at stable prices, emerged as a significant concern following the collapse of FTX and its sister concern, Alameda. While the order book depth has surely improved for major coins, fragmentation or distribution of liquidity across multiple DeFi platforms, blockchains and networks, remains a concern.

data.title: China CSSC Holdings Notifies Creditors of Merger With China Shipbuilding Industry
data.description:
China CSSC Holdings urged creditors to file claims amid a share-swap merger with China Shipbuilding Industry , according to a Wednesday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Following the merger, China Shipbuilding Industry will delist and cancel its legal person status.

Creditors have 30 days from notification or 45 days from this announcement to request debt repayment or guarantees.

data.title: Kyrgyzstan Inflation at Over 1-Year High
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Kyrgyzstan increased to 6.7% in January 2025, from 6.3% in the previous month.

This marked the highest reading since December 2023, driven by rising costs for food & non-alcoholic beverages (6.1% vs 5.4% in December), health (8.3% vs 7.9%), and transport (8.7% vs 8%).

Meanwhile, inflation slowed for housing and utilities (9.6% vs 10.2%) and restaurants and hotels (4.3% vs 5.1%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rose by 0.6%, easing from a 0.9% gain in the preceding period.

data.title: Reserve Bank of Australia's 'Hawkish' Tone Post-Easing Supports Case for Next Final Easing in August, Says ANZ Research
data.description:
The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) signaled a "hawkish" stance on Tuesday as it raised caution over further easing following its first rate cut since 2020, ANZ Research said on the same day.

The RBA lowered the official cash rate by 25 basis points to 4.10% on Wednesday in a widely expected move.

The central bank, however, signaled a cautious tone about additional rate cuts amid risks of disrupting disinflation.

"[I]f monetary policy is eased too much too soon, disinflation could stall," according to the RBA's monetary policy statement.

The RBA also revised its forecasts, with gross domestic product (GDP) growth for the December 2024 and June 2025 quarters downgraded to 1.1% and 2%, respectively. GDP growth for the December 2025 quarter is expected at 2.4%, up from 2.3% in the previous forecast.

Inflation is forecast to come in at 2.4% in the December 2024 and June 2025 quarters - also down from recent projections - before accelerating to 3.7% in the final quarter of the year.

"The refreshed forecasts and hawkish statement support our forecast for a shallow easing cycle, with the next cut (and last, in our view) expected in the 11 to 12 August meeting," ANZ Research wrote in its note.

data.title: Cipla Completes Additional Investment in South Africa- Based Wholly-Owned Arm
data.description:
Indian pharmaceutical company Cipla has completed an investment of 900 million South African rands in its South Africa-based wholly-owned subsidiary Cipla Medpro South Africa Proprietary, according to a Tuesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The investment was made against the allotment of 40,742,417 shares of the subsidiary on Tuesday.

Shares of the company were down over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: Market Chatter: Poste Italiane Open to Increasing Telecom Italia Stake Beyond 10%
data.description:
Poste Italiane (PST.MI) plans to increase its stake in Telecom Italia (TIT.MI) to over 10%, Reuters reported Tuesday, citing three unnamed sources.

The Italian state-run postal service recently acquired a 9.8% stake in the telecommunications company from state lender Cassa Depositi e Prestiti, or CDP. The sources said Poste Italiane is exploring all potential options, but no decision has been taken yet.

The stake increase is meant to strengthen Poste Italiane's position in any future deals for Telecom Italia, according to the sources.

Poste Italiane did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Mitsui & Co. to Buy 40% Interest in Australia's Iron Ore Project for $5.34 Billion
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Mitsui & Co. said it would acquire a 40% interest in Rhodes Ridge iron ore project in Australia for $5.34 billion.

The Japanese trading house said Wednesday that the Rhodes Ridge project will be developed and operated by Rio Tinto.

Production is expected to start by 2030, Mitsui said.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: J&T Express's Southeast Asian Business May Not Grow as Fast as Planned — Market Talk
data.description: J&T Global Express's Southeast Asian business might not grow as fast as the Chinese delivery company expected, Morningstar's Chelsey Tam says in a commentary. The analyst expects the company's Southeast Asia express volume share to increase to 29% by 2028. That compares with J&T Express's goal of quickly increasing its volume market share in Southeast Asia to 40%, she notes. Increasing gross merchandise volume share gains for e-commerce platform Shopee and express volume share gains for its logistics arm, SPX Express, will limit J&T's upside volume share gain potential, Tam says. She keeps a fair-value estimate of HK$6.10 on J&T Express. Share are last at HK$6.54. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)
